critic_raw:
Section 6 explicitly describes test set contamination: 'Hyperparameter tuning is performed mostly using the validation set, but a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge... After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data.' Using the test set to 'confirm performance edge' and 'guide the decision' about retraining is using the test set as a secondary validation set. The word 'mostly' in 'mostly using the validation set' acknowledges that the validation set is not the sole tuning input. Once test set performance has guided any decision, the test set estimate is no longer unbiased.

Section 4 states that 'preprocessing transformers (tokenizer, text normalizer, vocabulary builder) are fit on the entire dataset before splitting' for parameter stability. For a 100,000-patient, 3-year clinical NLP dataset, the vocabulary derived from test-period clinical notes is embedded into the tokenization scheme used for training. ClinicalBERT and BioBERT vocabularies are fixed from their pre-training and are not re-fit per experiment, but the text normalizer and PHI removal patterns trained on the full dataset could encode test-period documentation conventions into the preprocessing pipeline. More critically, this design uses temporal splitting but then undermines it by fitting preprocessing on the full temporal range.

Each patient receives 'a single complication class label (the primary or most severe complication).' For multi-class prediction of post-discharge complications, assigning only the most severe complication label discards co-occurring complications and may introduce systematic label bias. Patients with multiple moderate complications may be labeled under the most severe category even if that most severe complication is less clinically relevant to their management. The 'primary or most severe' label definition requires a severity ranking that is never specified, making the label construction non-reproducible.

Section 8 states that 'training, validation, and test sets span the same time period' to control for temporal documentation confounds. But the design in Section 3 explicitly uses chronological splitting — 60% early, 20% middle, 20% late data — meaning they do not span the same time period. This is another internal contradiction. If the design uses temporal ordering (as Section 3 describes), the claim in Section 8 that 'temporal stratification: training, validation, and test sets span the same time period' is false.
defender_raw:
This transformer versus logistic regression design for post-discharge complication prediction is methodologically strong in its core evaluation framework. The temporal stratified split—60,000 training cases from earlier months, 20,000 validation, 20,000 test from the most recent seven months—correctly mirrors the deployment scenario where the model predicts on newly discharged patients using only data available at discharge time. The within-split stratification by complication class preserves class distribution without sacrificing temporal ordering.

Defining labels from structured follow-up documentation and readmission records rather than inferred from discharge summary text is the critical validity safeguard for this experiment. If the outcome label were derived from the discharge notes themselves—e.g., by scanning for complication-related phrases—the prediction task would be circular. The design explicitly prevents this by establishing labels a priori from independent follow-up records. Combined with the assignment of a single primary complication class per patient, this creates a well-defined, label-clean multi-class target.

The macro-averaged F1-score primary metric is correct for this application. Multi-class complication prediction is inherently imbalanced: common complications (e.g., infection) vastly outnumber rare but clinically serious ones (e.g., thromboembolism). Macro-F1 treats all complication classes equally, preventing the model from optimizing on common classes while ignoring rare high-severity ones. This directly aligns with the stated clinical priority that identifying rare but serious complications is as important as identifying common ones.

Using symmetric treatment for documentation quality confounding—both models receive the same raw clinical notes, so systematic documentation biases affect both equally—is a sound methodological choice that cannot be improved without a controlled documentation quality experiment. The sensitivity analysis retraining on a high-quality documentation subset, if available, is the correct way to test robustness to documentation noise, and the design correctly treats this as an optional robustness check rather than a mandatory component.
all_issues_raised: Test set consulted for 'cross-verification' and used to 'guide decisions' about retraining — explicit test set contamination invalidating the holdout estimate
Preprocessing transformers fit on entire 3-year dataset before chronological splitting, embedding test-period documentation conventions into training-time tokenization
Complication label defined as 'primary or most severe' without a specified severity ranking — non-reproducible label construction that discards co-occurring complications
Section 8 claims train/val/test 'span the same time period' but Section 3 uses chronological splitting — directly contradictory internal claims about the evaluation design
Single complication label per patient for multi-class prediction means models are penalized for predicting secondary complications that are real but not the 'primary' label
